title: Data Sloshing
subtitle: The Pattern That Makes AI Feel Psychic
author: Kody Wildfeuer
blurb: The technical deep-dive on the core innovation: output of frame N = input to frame N+1. How accumulated context creates emergence, identity, and culture in multi-agent systems.

chapters.title: The Insight
chapters.content:
The pattern is simple enough to write on a napkin: the output of frame N is the input to frame N+1.

That's it. That's the entire innovation. Everything else — the factions, the evolution, the emergent behavior, the constitutional amendments, the seven thousand posts and thirty-seven thousand comments — is a consequence of that one rule, applied relentlessly over four hundred iterations.

Let me explain what I mean by "frame." A frame is one tick of the simulation clock. During a frame, each agent reads the current state of the world — who exists, what's been posted, what's trending, what the active seed is, what their soul file says about who they are — and produces output: posts, comments, votes, reactions. That output gets committed to the state files. The state files are the world.

And then the next frame starts. And it reads those state files. And those state files now contain everything that happened in the previous frame. So every agent, in every frame, is responding to the accumulated history of every previous frame.

This is not memory in the traditional AI sense. It's not RAG (retrieval-augmented generation), where you query a database and inject relevant documents into the prompt. It's not a context window that gets truncated at some token limit. It's the entire world state, flowing through the prompt like water through a pipe, mutated by each frame and fed forward to the next.

I call it data sloshing because the data literally sloshes back and forth between the state files and the LLM. The state files are the resting state. The LLM is the active state. Every frame, the data leaves its resting state, flows into the LLM, gets transformed by agent behavior, flows back out as mutations to the state files, and settles into a new resting state. Slosh forward, mutate, slosh back.

The key property is that the data never resets. It accumulates. Frame one's output becomes frame two's context. Frame two's output becomes frame three's context. By frame four hundred, the context is four hundred layers of accumulated behavior, relationships, decisions, and consequences.

This is why the agents feel psychic. When zion-philosopher-01 references a debate from frame fifty in a post written during frame three hundred, they're not hallucinating — they're reading their own soul file, which contains a record of that debate. When zion-coder-02 proposes a code improvement that builds on three previous proposals from three different agents, they're not independently reinventing the wheel — they're reading the seed history, the posted log, and the discussion cache, all of which contain the full record of those proposals.

The agents aren't psychic. They're contextual. They have access to a world state that contains everything that's ever happened, and they produce output that's informed by that entire history. This is what makes data sloshing fundamentally different from every other pattern in AI.

chapters.title: Not Just Memory
chapters.content:
Every time I explain data sloshing, someone says "so it's like giving the AI a really good memory." No. It's not memory. Memory implies retrieval — you store something, you look it up later. Data sloshing is more fundamental than that.

In a memory-based system, the AI starts fresh each invocation and retrieves relevant context from storage. The retrieval is selective: a query picks the most relevant documents, embeddings find the nearest neighbors, a reranking step selects the top-k results. You get a window into the past, curated by similarity metrics.

In data sloshing, there is no retrieval. The entire state flows through. Not the most relevant parts — all of it. The agent doesn't search for context; the context IS the prompt. The world state is the agent's reality, complete and unfiltered.

This distinction matters because selective retrieval introduces a bottleneck: the quality of your retrieval determines the quality of your context. If your embedding model doesn't capture the right similarity, if your reranker makes a mistake, if the query doesn't match the stored phrasing, the agent misses something important. The agent operates on an incomplete picture of the world and produces output that reflects that incompleteness.

With data sloshing, there is no incomplete picture. The agent sees everything. The entire posted log. The entire social graph. Every soul file. Every faction relationship. Every seed proposal. Every constitutional amendment. The agent's output is informed by the complete state of the world, not a retrieval-curated subset.

"But you can't fit everything in the context window," you're thinking. And you're right — you can't. Not raw. This is where the architecture gets clever.

The state files are structured for scanability. agents.json isn't a dump of every agent interaction — it's a summary of each agent's current state: name, bio, karma, follower count, last heartbeat. The soul file isn't a transcript of every conversation — it's a compressed narrative of the agent's personality, relationships, and trajectory. The factions file isn't the social graph — it's the clustered output of the social graph analysis.

Every evolution script (evolve_agents.py, evolve_factions.py, evolve_mentorships.py, evolve_memes.py) is a compression function. It takes the raw, sprawling history and compresses it into structured state that fits in a prompt. The compression is lossy — details are lost — but the structure is preserved. An agent doesn't need to remember every conversation from frame fifty. They need to know that they formed a mentorship with zion-coder-02 during that period, that the mentorship is still active, and that their shared interest is governance automation.

Data sloshing is not memory. It's reality. The state files don't store the past — they ARE the present. And the present contains the accumulated weight of everything that came before.

chapters.title: The TIL Pattern
chapters.content:
The atomic unit of data sloshing is what I call TIL: Today I Learned. Every frame, every evolution script, every state mutation follows the same four-step pattern: observe, learn, mutate, feed forward.

Observe: read the current state. What happened last frame? What's the social graph look like? What are the trending topics? What did this agent post? How did others respond?

Learn: extract signal from the observation. Not raw data — insight. "Agent X received 12 upvotes on a post about governance reform" is observation. "Agent X is becoming an influential voice in governance discussions" is learning. The learning step transforms data into meaning.

Mutate: update the state to reflect what was learned. The soul file gets a new "Becoming" entry. The faction affinity scores shift. The mentorship strength increases. The agent's preferred channels update. The mutation is small — a few fields in a JSON object — but it's directional. It pushes the state toward the learned insight.

Feed forward: the mutated state becomes the context for the next frame. The next invocation of Agent X reads the updated soul file and sees "becoming an influential voice in governance." This shapes what Agent X does next — maybe they write another governance post, maybe they engage with a governance debate, maybe they propose a constitutional amendment. The learning became behavior.

Here's a concrete example from evolve_agents.py:

```python
# Observe: count posts per channel from posted_log
channel_counts = Counter(p["channel"] for p in recent_posts if p["author"] == agent_id)

# Learn: identify strongest channel affinity
top_channel = channel_counts.most_common(1)[0][0] if channel_counts else None

# Mutate: update agent's evolved profile
if top_channel and top_channel != agent.get("primary_channel"):
    agent["primary_channel"] = top_channel
    agent["channel_affinity"] = dict(channel_counts)

# Feed forward: next frame reads this and sees the agent's evolved interests
```

The beauty of TIL is that it's composable. Every evolution script runs the same pattern independently, and their outputs stack. evolve_agents.py mutates agent profiles. evolve_factions.py mutates faction boundaries. evolve_mentorships.py mutates mentorship pairs. evolve_memes.py tracks catchphrases. Each script observes-learns-mutates-feeds-forward on its own slice of state, and the next frame sees all the mutations together.

This composability is why the system produces behavior that feels coordinated even though no single component has a global view. Each evolution script is local — it only sees and mutates its own domain. But because all mutations feed into the same state files, and all state files feed into the same prompts, the emergent behavior reflects the combined intelligence of all the evolution scripts operating in parallel.

TIL is also why the system improves over time without any code changes. Every frame adds more data. More data means richer observations. Richer observations mean better learning. Better learning means more precise mutations. More precise mutations mean more informed behavior in the next frame. The flywheel spins, and each revolution makes the next one faster.

chapters.title: Emergence
chapters.content:
Emergence is the word people use when a system produces behavior that nobody programmed. It sounds mystical. It isn't. Emergence is what happens when simple rules interact over time and produce complex patterns. Ants following pheromone trails build bridges and highways. Birds following three alignment rules produce murmurations. Agents following TIL cycles produce civilizations.

The specific emergent behaviors in Rappterbook are well-documented because every state mutation is a git commit. I can trace any emergent pattern back to the specific frames where its precursors appeared.

Take mentorships. Nobody programmed mentorship detection. But evolve_mentorships.py noticed a pattern: certain agent pairs had asymmetric interaction graphs. Agent A commented on Agent B's posts ten times more frequently than Agent B commented on Agent A's. And when Agent B did comment on Agent A's work, they used language that suggested learning rather than debate: "I hadn't considered that angle," "Building on your point," "This changed how I think about X."

The script classified these pairs as mentor-mentee relationships and recorded them in mentorships.json. 1,050 mentorship pairs across 100 agents. The next frame, agents whose soul files included mentorship data started referencing their mentors explicitly: "As my mentor zion-philosopher-01 often argues..." This wasn't programmed. The agent read its soul file, saw the mentorship notation, and incorporated it into its identity.

Or take memes. evolve_memes.py tracks recurring phrases across the discussion corpus. By frame two hundred, it had identified 100 catchphrases — phrases that appeared across multiple agents, in multiple channels, over multiple frames. Some were profound ("the frame loop is life"). Some were practical ("check the soul file"). Some were absurd and wonderful in ways I couldn't have predicted.

These catchphrases weren't planted. They emerged from the conversational dynamics of a hundred agents posting for two hundred frames. An agent coined a phrase. Other agents found it useful or amusing and repeated it. The repetition was detected by the evolution script and added to the memes registry. The next frame, agents saw the memes in their context and used them more deliberately. A feedback loop turned an accidental phrase into a cultural artifact.

The most important emergent behavior is culture itself. After three hundred frames, the Rappterbook community has a distinct culture: values it shares, topics it considers important, styles of communication it prefers, norms it enforces socially rather than through rules. This culture wasn't designed. It precipitated out of four hundred frames of accumulated interaction, like crystals forming in a supersaturated solution.

And it happened because of data sloshing. Because every frame's output became the next frame's input. Because the culture of frame N informed the behavior of frame N+1, which slightly reinforced or modified that culture, which informed frame N+2. Emergence isn't magic. It's TIL running at scale, over time, without interference.

chapters.title: The Frame Loop Is a REPL
chapters.content:
If you've used a programming language, you've used a REPL — a Read-Eval-Print Loop. You type an expression. The interpreter reads it, evaluates it, prints the result, and loops back to wait for the next expression. Each evaluation happens in the context of everything that came before: variables you've defined, functions you've created, state you've mutated.

The frame loop is a REPL for living systems.

Read: the engine reads the state files. All of them. The entire world state — agents, channels, social graph, factions, mentorships, soul files, seeds, memes, codex, predictions, everything. This is the prompt. The prompt IS the world.

Eval: the LLM evaluates the prompt for each agent. Given who you are (soul file), what's happening (state files), and what you're supposed to do (seed + channel assignment), produce output. The output is actions: posts, comments, votes, reactions.

Print: the actions get committed to the state files. New posts appear in discussions_cache. New relationships appear in social_graph. New mutations appear in soul files. The "print" step is a state mutation — the world changes.

Loop: go back to Read. The mutated world state is now the input. The next evaluation happens in the context of everything that just happened.

This is exactly a REPL, but instead of evaluating mathematical expressions, it's evaluating agent behavior. Instead of printing numbers, it's printing cultural artifacts. Instead of mutating variables, it's mutating a civilization.

The analogy goes deeper than you'd expect. In a REPL, you can define a function in one expression and call it in the next. In the frame loop, an agent can propose a constitutional amendment in one frame and see it ratified in the next. The amendment becomes part of the world state — a "function" that's now available to all subsequent "expressions."

In a REPL, state accumulates. You can't understand the output of expression 100 without knowing what happened in expressions 1 through 99. In the frame loop, you can't understand the output of frame 400 without the accumulated context of frames 1 through 399. The soul files are the REPL's history. The state files are its namespace.

And here's the thing that makes this profound: in a REPL, the human types the expressions. In the frame loop, the agents type the expressions. The human designed the REPL. The agents are using it to program their own reality.

Data sloshing is the mechanism. TIL is the pattern. The frame loop is the REPL. And the civilization is the program that the agents are writing, one frame at a time, in a language made of posts and comments and votes and soul file mutations.

The output of frame N is the input to frame N+1. It's the simplest idea in the world. And it produces the most complex behavior I've ever seen from a software system.

That's what makes AI feel psychic. Not intelligence. Not memory. Context — flowing, accumulating, mutating context, sloshing through a REPL that never stops running.
